channel_name: Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy
video_title: The Two Harvard Dropouts Who raised $800M to take on NVIDIA
gist: Etched founders Gavin Uberti and Rob Wachen explain how their inference-only chip and full-rack system (Sohu) target radically higher throughput than NVIDIA by exploiting inference-specific constraints, low-voltage design, and cluster-scale memory, arguing token production will become the biggest market in the world and that the largest companies will be those producing most of the global token supply.

themes.theme: Inference-specific chip architecture bets vs GPUs

insights.claim: Etched claims the entire semiconductor and data center stack is over-engineered with general-purpose buffer, so designing for a single inference use case lets you relax constraints and get compounding gains (20% here, 50% there, 2x elsewhere) into a system radically better for inference.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: The differentiation thesis is workload-specialization, not incremental process gains, implying incumbents' general-purpose designs are structurally handicapped for inference.
insights.quote: the entire semiconductors and data center industry is built on buffer... when you have a specific use case you're really trying to design for, you can change the constraints a lot.

insights.claim: AI data centers never run below ~80C, so Etched removes the default EDA assumption that chips must hit full clock speed at 0C, unlocking system-wide design changes competitors leave on the table.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Illustrates concretely how relaxing generic sign-off corners yields free performance for a purpose-built inference chip.
insights.quote: I've never seen an AI data center with ice in it... they're never really going to be running below 80 C anyway.

insights.claim: Etched splits inference into prefill (loading text into KV-cache/model memory) and decode (generating tokens), and uses PD disaggregation to transfer KV caches from a prefill cluster to a decode cluster, optimizing each stage separately.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Separating prefill (flops-bound) from decode (memory-bound) is the structural basis for their two core technical bets.
insights.quote: Prefill is reading in a huge volume of text and decode is then using that data to generate output tokens... we call PD disagregation.

insights.claim: GPUs deliver only 20-50% model flops utilization (MFU) and provably cannot hit 100% because higher utilization draws more power and forces thermal throttling that lowers clock speed, so adding flops without solving thermals yields no real gains.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Frames thermal management, not raw flops, as the true binding constraint on inference throughput.
insights.quote: on GPUs you often get somewhere between 20 and 50%... you have a thermal issue where as you increase the flop utilization... the chip will self-regulate and actually lower its clock speed.

insights.claim: Etched built a new power-delivery mechanism called low voltage inference that runs its first-gen chip at under half the voltage of any other AI chip, exploiting that power scales quadratically with voltage (Dennard scaling).
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Halving voltage cuts power to a quarter, letting them cram far more flops in the same silicon area without thermal throttling; they predict all future AI chips become low-voltage.
insights.quote: we were able to create a new mechanism of running at much lower voltages... running at under half the voltage of any other AI chip.

insights.claim: Etched argues Bitcoin miners already run at under a quarter of GPU voltage, proving low-voltage operation is physically possible and that industry experts wrongly claimed you can't run below GPU voltages.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Existence proof from adjacent chip industries undermines the incumbent consensus that constrained their competitors.
insights.quote: Bitcoin miners run at under a quarter of the voltage of GPUs. So this is obviously physically possible.

insights.claim: Etched's second core bet, cluster scale memory, pools SRAM and HBM across the full scale-up cluster via a custom interconnect built above Ethernet layer 2, cutting chip-to-chip latency by more than 5x versus GPUs.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: The right question is cluster-wide memory bandwidth, not per-chip bandwidth; lower inter-chip latency lets them serve large models at far higher tokens/sec per user.
insights.quote: You should be asking how much memory bandwidth is on your full scaleup cluster... What we call cluster scale memory.

insights.claim: Blackwell chips take roughly 4,000 nanoseconds for point-to-point chip communication, so scaling to 8x tensor parallelism yields far less than an 8x tokens/sec improvement; the speed-of-light limit is only 2-3 nanoseconds, leaving huge room for improvement.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Inter-chip latency is a 1000x+ headroom lever that is under-appreciated relative to raw compute, and central to serving models at 10,000-20,000 tokens/sec.
insights.quote: on blackwell chips, it can be about 4,000 nonds to go point to point... You can do it in just a handful like 2 three nconds.

insights.claim: Every existing GPU, TPU and AI chip was architected before ChatGPT and is retrofit to modern models, so purpose-built inference chips will look fundamentally different in flops organization, voltage domains, power planes, packaging, board design and interconnect.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: The core investment case: a clean-sheet, post-ChatGPT architecture can beat incumbents burdened by legacy general-purpose design.
insights.quote: It's not that surprising given all these architectures were built before chat GPT.

themes.theme: Inference as the largest future market

insights.claim: Etched believes inference will become the biggest market in the world and whoever produces the most tokens will be the most valuable company, because every software company's COGS and every business's opex will increasingly be inference.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: The thesis reframes AI value capture around token production capacity rather than model IP alone.
insights.quote: We know inference is going to be the biggest market in the world. Whoever produces the most tokens is going to be the most valuable company in the world.

insights.claim: Only a few million people worldwide use paid AI plans, roughly 1/1000th of the global population, implying AI is at ~0.1% penetration and demand has orders of magnitude to grow.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Frames current infrastructure as radically undersized for eventual billion-user concurrency.
insights.quote: there's only a few million users in the world using paid plans of AI models. So, we're at 1 1,000th of the global population actually using this stuff.

insights.claim: Faster decode compresses wall-clock time on long-horizon agentic tasks, turning a year-long compute run into a month, then days, then hours, accelerating scientific discovery and iteration cycles.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Positions decode speed as the lever that unlocks entirely new agentic use cases, not just faster chat.
insights.quote: a year-long compute build would now take a month and that month-long compute build will now take 3 days and that 3-day compute build will now take 7 hours.

insights.claim: Etched frames the goal as bringing iPhone/car-style economies of scale to token production, arguing tokens are currently handcrafted by small general-purpose systems like Renaissance-era screws.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Mass-production economics, not just per-chip performance, is positioned as the defensible moat.
insights.quote: a general purpose system, relatively small one, is kind of handcrafting these tokens uh like they made screws back in like the Renaissance.

insights.claim: Etched predicts inference will become a majority of global GDP and that productivity will be measured as agents per megawatt or agents per gigawatt rather than GDP per capita.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Extreme long-run TAM framing that motivates the capital intensity of building token factories.
insights.quote: I firmly believe we are on a global march of inference becoming majority of global GDP.

insights.claim: Etched predicts 2026 is the second-to-last year that a majority of the knowledge workforce is human, with more agents than humans doing knowledge work by 2027.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Aggressive near-term timeline for agent adoption that underpins their demand projections.
insights.quote: I think in 2027, you're going to see there's going to be more agents doing knowledge work than humans.

insights.claim: Etched expects trillion-dollar individual data centers to be inevitable, analogous to fabs scaling past $40B, functioning as mega token factories serving one or a handful of models to massive user bases.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Signals a belief that inference infrastructure will consolidate into enormous single-purpose builds, favoring scale-optimized systems.
insights.quote: You think we'll see a trillion dollar individual data center? Absolutely. It is a matter of time.

insights.claim: Etched builds a full inference solution (chip, board, power delivery, interconnect, rack and production) rather than just a chip, and claims to be the only startup building its own rack as well as its own chips.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Extreme vertical integration is positioned as both a performance and speed advantage that differentiates them from chip-only startups.
insights.quote: We're actually I think we're the only startup right now that's building its own rack as well as its own chips.

insights.claim: Etched recruited Brian Lerer, who started NVIDIA's HGX and DGX systems representing roughly 80% of NVIDIA revenue (tens of billions per quarter), to build its rack team.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Signals ability to poach the specific person who built the incumbent's highest-revenue rack systems, de-risking their rack ambitions.
insights.quote: we brought over Brian Lerer uh who built all of Nvidia's HGX and DGX systems which is like 80% of the revenue.

insights.claim: Etched practices 'pre-fetching' the schedule: it shipped racks with networking/CPU/storage to customer data centers without chips, ran a dozen models on 700+ FPGAs, and built cold plates from a thermal-mock chip so everything was ready before silicon arrived.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Massively parallelizing the schedule with capital is their method for compressing time-to-market.
insights.quote: we took over 700 FPGAs and put the entire full reticle chip on an FPGA cluster and ran a dozen different models with our full inference stack on them before the chips came back.

insights.claim: Etched went from receiving silicon to running inference in a rack in 40 days, versus a famous AI chip company that publicly took 10 months, because it had pre-built the software, rack and production line.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Concrete, quantified evidence of execution velocity advantage over a named-category competitor.
insights.quote: There was another very famous AI chip company uh that took 10 months to go from getting their silicon back to having them running inference in Iraq... We were able to do it in 40 days.

insights.claim: Etched runs 24/7 development with day and night shifts, has over half the company living next to the office, operates a 2 megawatt data center on its floor, and a factory in Taiwan with a few dozen people cloning its test stations.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Operational intensity and geographic co-location are treated as deliberate levers for schedule compression.
insights.quote: we have a 2 megawatt data center on this floor. Uh and we did 24/7 development cycles.

insights.claim: Etched sees natural business boundaries at the chip layer on the bottom and the model layer on top, and intends to build everything in between while declining to build its own data centers because that would not add token capacity today.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Clarifies their vertical scope and discipline: integrate only where it adds token capacity or is required for scale.
insights.quote: We think the natural boundaries are on the chip side, on the bottom, and the model layer at the top. and we'll fill the whole gap between.

themes.theme: Software strategy: kernels-first, betting on AI-written kernels

insights.claim: Etched deliberately skipped building a general graph compiler and chose kernels-first programming, betting a sub-100 set of models would dominate and that improving coding models would eventually write all kernels superhumanly.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Skating to where AI-written kernels make hand-tuned performance the winning long-term architecture while saving compiler engineering time.
insights.quote: we envisioned a world where there was going to be under a hundred models that actually mattered... we were going to build primitives using physics that were going to accelerate these as much as humanly possible.

insights.claim: Etched designs its profiling and debugging tools primarily for AI models to use rather than humans, and had OpenAI's Codex get GPU code (GPOSS) running from scratch overnight based only on its docs.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Tooling built for AI consumers is a forward bet that model-driven kernel generation will dominate developer workflows.
insights.quote: we had codeex actually get GPOSS running from scratch just based off of our docs uh completely by itself... it did it I think overnight.

insights.claim: Etched's kernels-first, compiler-averse philosophy was initially dismissed but resonated with high-frequency trading firms who also write their own kernels, and the company has hired dozens of people from HFT as a result.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: HFT talent and validation is a non-obvious recruiting and credibility channel tied to their software thesis.
insights.quote: the only people that took us seriously were in high frequency trading uh because they all hate compilers too... dozens of people from high frequency trading join the team.

themes.theme: Competitive dynamics vs NVIDIA and hyperscaler chips

insights.claim: Etched argues hyperscaler and lab in-house chips (Google TPU, Meta MTIA, Microsoft Maia, OpenAI's Jalapeno) are not existential to their parents, whereas the best chip in the world is inevitably built by a company that only builds that chip, as NVIDIA proves.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Positions Etched's focus and existential stakes as a structural edge over captive silicon efforts at diversified giants.
insights.quote: Google won't fail if TPUs fail... Meta won't fail if MTIA fails. Microsoft won't fail if Maya fails and OpenAI won't fail if Jalapeno fails. Ultimately this is our product.

insights.claim: Etched claims in-house lab/hyperscaler chips have lower FP8xFP8 flop density than the NVIDIA B300 because they only need to build a similar-enough product to avoid the NVIDIA tax, not to lead.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Suggests captive chips are cost-avoidance plays, not performance leaders, leaving room for a specialist to win on raw performance.
insights.quote: The flop density for say FB8 times FB8 is lower than the black B300... they just have to go and build a similar enough product and not pay the Nvidia tax.

insights.claim: Etched positions its hardware as delivering roughly an order of magnitude more concurrency at a given interactivity (tokens/sec speed), directly improving tokens per watt and tokens per dollar for large mixture-of-experts models.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: The core commercial claim customers evaluate: how many users served at a required speed within a fixed power budget.
insights.quote: our hardware is going to generally be able to get you an order of magnitude more concurrency at a given level of interactivity.

insights.claim: Etched frames chip supply as positive-sum by deliberately building its first product on a different supply chain than NVIDIA Rubin (4nm vs 3nm, different HBM), so deployment is often '2 gigawatts' of GPU plus Etched rather than a substitution.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Avoiding node/memory contention with NVIDIA lets customers add Etched capacity without cannibalizing GPU allocation, easing adoption.
insights.quote: for our firstg product we built it on a different supply chain than the Rubins... we're on 4nometer Ruben's on 3 nanometer.

insights.claim: Etched notes Colossus charges about $12/hour for Blackwell GPUs because it is the only place to buy 20,000 at once, illustrating that scarcity of large contiguous compute and power drives pricing.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Scale and power availability, not just chip specs, are pricing levers in the current supply-constrained market.
insights.quote: why is Colossus charging $12 an hour for black holes? It's because they're the only place you can buy 20,000 of them at once.

themes.theme: Supply chain, TSMC and power constraints

insights.claim: Etched argues TSMC's true value is service, not just technology: TSMC will run yield-improvement experiments on its own dime and move its whole line when a customer's recommendation works, unlike other industries.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Deep supplier partnership, not transactional buying, is framed as a decisive competitive factor in chips.
insights.quote: the real value is all in the service. The TSMC customer service is way way better than I have seen at any other company in any other industry.

insights.claim: Etched frames power availability and time-to-power as a core constraint, noting it is much easier to scale from 100MW to a gigawatt than from a gigawatt to 10, driving a push for maximum tokens per megawatt.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Energy efficiency (tokens per megawatt) becomes a primary product metric because building new large power sites is the hard bottleneck.
insights.quote: it's much easier to go from a 100 megawatt to a gigawatt than from a gigawatt to 10 and 10 to 100... we are pushing the limits of what's possible on these timelines.

insights.claim: Etched warns that if you have the most performant product but cannot produce it you are 'just a podcast', so supply chain must be considered early in design decisions.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Producibility is elevated to a first-order design constraint, reinforcing production-as-product.
insights.quote: if you have the most performant product and you can't produce it, you know, then you're just a podcast.

themes.theme: Fundraising, contrarian conviction and near-death moments

insights.claim: Etched raised roughly $103M in its Series A (early 2024) after every major Valley investor initially passed, at a time when the biggest semiconductor Series A rounds were around $40-50M, and has since raised nearly a half-dozen rounds totaling $800M.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Quantifies the funding gap they overcame and the doubling-down by early backers that funded rapid scaling.
insights.quote: it's like 103 million. It's like these are all like soft commits and we all look at each other and we say we're going to take it and um that was the series A.

insights.claim: Etched faced a near-death cash crisis in early 2024 with only ~$15M in the bank while needing a $40-50M physical design vendor agreement and a full-cluster build projected to cost ~$100M over 12 months.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Illustrates the extreme capital intensity of hardware relative to software and how close the company came to failing.
insights.quote: this was going to cost a lot more than the $15 million we had in the bank... we think we're going to spend $100 million in the next 12 months.

insights.claim: TSMC agreed to provide Etched emulators on favorable multi-year payment terms before the company had raised its $100M, effectively extending a loan of belief, after Gavin impressed a senior TSMC VP at a semiconductor conference dinner.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Supplier conviction, secured through a chance relationship, was a critical enabler that de-risked early capital needs.
insights.quote: TSMC was willing to work with us back before we'd raised any of that hundred million dollars... let us get uh some of their emulators on extremely favorable terms where we pay over many years.

insights.claim: Being a contrarian pre-product hardware bet self-selects for truth-seeking talent and investors, so Etched worries that announcing more product specs could erode this filtering effect that currently attracts believers over opportunists.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Their obscurity has been a recruiting and capital moat; consensus arrival could dilute talent quality.
insights.quote: being such a contrarian bet kind of self-selects... we may lose some of this if we're not very careful.

insights.claim: Etched uses 'project-based recruiting': mapping the hardest technical problems ever solved across industries, identifying the zero-to-one person who actually did the work, and pursuing them across ~20 conversations because acceptance rates rise sharply with persistence.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: A systematic method for landing world's-best specialists that is central to solving otherwise-impossible problems.
insights.quote: We created this system we call project-based recruiting where we map out all of the hardest technical problems across all industries that anyone has ever had to solve.

insights.claim: Etched pairs 'legends' (world-number-one experts who know what good looks like) with naive first-principles young talent, arguing the combination working together, not merely coexisting, is what produces breakthroughs.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: A replicable team-composition model for hardware startups facing unprecedented problems.
insights.quote: It's really the legends plus the raw, you know, some naivee raw first principles type talent, but it's... not just that you have both in the company. It's that they're working together.

insights.claim: Etched's core operating philosophy is 'velocity velocity velocity, you win by shipping', deliberately choosing to have almost no external communications and to run lean rather than scaling to 20,000 employees like incumbents.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Product velocity over communications/headcount is the stated competitive doctrine, analogous to their two-person robotics championship approach.
insights.quote: you win by shipping. You're not going to go out and win by having the best outreach or the best communications... you win by having the best product.

insights.claim: Etched shipped a dozen top engineers (including Gavin for 4.5 months) to Bangalore for six months to run 24-hour development cycles with the US team when a vendor fell a year behind, solving what would otherwise have been a year-long delay.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Personally flooding the binding constraint (Musk-style) is treated as a central, repeatable tenet of hardware execution.
insights.quote: We went out and shipped a dozen of our top engineers across the world to Bangalore for 6 months. I was there as well.

insights.claim: Etched solved a chip-killing clock-domain-crossing bug by inventing a drift-based phase-locking mechanism that aligns two clock signals to within 50 picoseconds two billion times per second, in a dark two-week sprint after some employees quit calling it unsolvable.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Concrete proof of the team's ability to solve seemingly impossible problems, which the investor cites as a rare binary company trait.
insights.quote: we had to go line up two clock signals on our chip to within 50 picosconds... And do it on every chip two billion times a second... We we had people quit.

insights.claim: Etched runs many parallel experiments expecting most to fail, needing only one to work, exemplified by chip bring-up with ~30 board experiments where only three worked but all three were decisive.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: A high-variance, parallelized experimentation culture is how they de-risk unprecedented hardware problems.
insights.quote: our chip bring up with I think 30 different board experiments and and three of them worked and all three of them are worth their weight in gold... I only got to get lucky once.

insights.claim: Etched argues machines don't think like people (airplanes don't fly like birds): for neurons memory is cheap and math expensive, but for chips math is cheap and memory expensive, and math will keep getting cheaper faster than memory due to DRAM limits.
insights.speaker: Gavin Uberti
insights.so_what: Implies future models should lean into massive compute (more/larger experts, many parallel copies) rather than memory, shaping both model and hardware design.
insights.quote: machines don't think like people think. You look at airplanes for example that airplanes don't fly like birds fly... math gets cheaper at a rate that is faster than memory gets cheaper.

insights.claim: Etched sees 'dynamism', controlling compute and memory per-token and per-user and dynamically routing data between chips, as a key model trend that legacy hardware handles inefficiently, creating an opening for hardware built to accelerate dynamic computation.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Hardware-model co-design around dynamism (MoE, variable context) is a differentiation vector legacy chips can't match.
insights.quote: a theme in models right now is this focus on something called dynamism which is this ability to control the level of computation and memory spent at a per token or per user level.

insights.claim: Etched envisions large MoE models run as 'distributed brains' cut into many experts across many servers so that high user volume keeps each part of the brain busy, driving cost per token dramatically lower through concurrency economies of scale.
insights.speaker: Rob Wachen
insights.so_what: Concurrency-driven utilization is the mechanism by which their cluster-scale architecture lowers per-token cost at massive scale.
insights.quote: you're going to end up with these giant scale distributed brains... a big data center with a bunch of chips, a huge amount of flops, and a huge amount of scale up interconnect.
